rulings.Case Title: ITC FINANCIAL LICENSES, INC. VS. STAN SCHULDINER
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 28, 2018, Line 1, CROSS-DEFENDANTS ITC FINANCIAL LICENSES, INC. and THE BANCORP BANK's (INCORRECTLY SUED AS THE BANCORP, MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication Cross-defendants ITC Financial Licenses, Inc. and The Bancorp Bank's motion for summary judgment on the fifth amended cross-complaint filed by Stan Schuldiner for violations of the CLRA and UCL, fraud, and negligent misrepresentation is granted. The undisputed facts show that Mr. Schuldiner has not suffered any cognizable harm or damages to support any of his four claims. The UCL "requires that a plaintiff have 'lost money or property' to have standing to sue. The plain import of this is that a plaintiff now must demonstrate some form of economic injury." (Kwikset Corp. v. Sup. Crt. (2011) 51 Cal.4th 310, 323.) Per the CLRA, Mr. Schuldiner must show "actual injury," that is, "some kind of tangible increased cost or burden." (Bower v. AT&T Mobility, LLC (2011) 196 Cal.App.4th 1545, 1556.) Fraud and negligent misrepresentation claims also require proof of injury. (Id. at 1557.) Emotional distress damages are not recoverable on a misrepresentation claim. (O'Neil v. Spillane (1975) 45 Cal.App.3d 147, 159; Butler-Rupp v. Lourdeaux (2005) 134 Cal.App.4th 1220, 1227.) Mr. Schuldiner seeks "[a] refund, emotional distress, interest, attorney fees, statutory damages, punitives." (Schuldiner Depo. 28:6-9; 202:4-9.) Mr. Schuldiner acknowledges that the gift cards could be used at other retailers. (Schuldiner Depo. 132:18-21; 151:17-24.) When specifically questioned about his damages, Mr. Schuldiner cites "[n]ightmares, obsession, inability to focus" and merely refers to his conclusory list of damages. (Schuldiner Depo. 204:23-205:7.) The foregoing evidence presented by the ITC parties satisfies their summary judgment motion burden as it shows that Mr. Schuldiner has no cognizable damages as a matter of law. Mr. Schuldiner fails to present any evidence creating a triable issue that he suffered any actionable harm or incurred any recoverable damages. Mr. Schuldinder solely relies on his declaration and attachments to oppose the motion. Even if the ITC parties' objection to that declaration for failure to comply with the subscription requirement of CCP 2015.5 were overruled, the declaration fails to show that Mr. Schuldiner suffered any cognizable harm on any of his claims. There is no evidence that the gift cards are worth anything less than their face value. Mr. Schuldiner's request for a continuance for further discovery is denied. His declaration fails to meet the requirements of CCP 437c(h) and there is no indication that he will be able to obtain any facts that will allow him to defeat this motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for the ITC parties is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=302/hk

rulings.Case Title: RICHARD A DENTON VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 28, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's MOTION TO STRIKE 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendants City and County of San Francisco, Barbara Hale, John Doyle, and Lori Mitchell's motion to strike portions of the second amended complaint filed by plaintiff Richard Denton is: a) granted with 20 days leave to amend as to paragraph 62; b) granted without leave to amend as to the allegations in the eighth cause of action for failure to prevent discrimination against the individual defendants; and 3) denied as to all other portions of the second amended complaint sought to be stricken. Paragraphs 57-61 and 63-66 raise allegations similar to the allegations in the DFEH/EEOC charge. Per Okoli v. Lockheed Technical Operations Co. (1995) 36 Cal.App.4th 1607, Mr. Denton does not need to return to the DFEH every time there is a new incident of discrimination that is similar to what has previously occurred. Paragraph 62 refers to an accommodation request by Mr. Denton following his surgery. Mr. Denton has leave to clarify that the request was related to the medical condition referred to in the DFEH/EEOC charge. The harassment allegations withstand a motion to strike for the same reason that the harassment cause of action withstands defendants' demurrer. The failure to prevent discrimination allegations alleged against Barbara Hale, John Doyle, and Lori Mitchell are stricken because a failure to prevent discrimination claim can only be asserted against an employer. Defendants have withdrawn the portion of their motion seeking to strike defamation allegations. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: RICHARD A DENTON VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 28, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANTs CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, BARBARA HALE, JOHN DOYLE and LORI MITCHELL's DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendants City and County of San Francisco, Barbara Hale, John Doyle, and Lori Mitchell's demurrer to the sixth cause of action for violation of the Medical Information Act and the seventh cause of action for harassment in the second amended complaint filed by plaintiff Richard Denton is sustained with 20 days leave to amend as to the sixth cause of action and overruled as to the seventh cause of action. The request for judicial notice of the April 16, 2013 government claim is granted. On its face that claim suggests that Mr. Denton's CMIA cause of action accrued in July of 2012 and Mr. Denton filed an untimely government claim more than six months later. Mr. Denton is given leave to amend to allege specific facts to support application of the discovery rule to render his government claim timely. "In order to rely on the discovery rule for delayed accrual of a cause of action, '[a] plaintiff whose complaint shows on its face that his claim would be barred without the benefit of the discovery rule must specifically plead facts to show (1) the time and manner of discovery and (2) the inability to have made earlier discovery despite reasonable diligence.'"(Fox v. Ethicon Endo-Surgery, Inc. (2005) 35 Cal.4th 797, 808(citation omitted.)). Defendants do not cite any authority that Mr. Denton needed to seek leave to present a late claim. Rason v. Santa Barbara City Housing Authority (1988) 201 Cal.App.3d 817, 822 indicates otherwise. The scope of Mr. Denton's permissible causes of action is limited not by the March 12, 2013 DFEH/EEOC charge, but by the scope of the DFEH/EEOC investigation which can reasonably be expected to grow out of the charge. Mr. Denton's harassment cause of action is "like or reasonably related to" the claims that were expressly stated in the charge. The harassment cause of action is viable because it would reasonably have been uncovered in an investigation of the charges that were made. (Okoli v. Lockheed Technical Operations Co. (1995) 36 Cal.App.4th 1607.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: ROBERT ROBINSON ET AL VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION / Notice Of Motion & Motion To Challenge Confidentiality Designation Of Documents Subject To Stipulated Non Sharing Protective Order * Redacted *
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 28, 2018, Line 1, PLAINTIFF JORDAN MEDINA'S Motion To Challenge Confidentiality Designation Of Documents Subject To Stipulated Non Sharing Protective Order * Redacted * Pro Tem Judge Stephanie Krmpotic, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is continued to September 12, 2018. Plaintiff's motion papers are incomplete. The moving party is required to immediately provide courtesy copies of his filings with room 301 (Discovery) with a cover letter reflecting the new hearing date per SF Local Rule 2.7B. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to JSKrmpotic@LOWBALL.COM with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ALTEN CONSTRUCTION, INC. VS. FAST WRAP RENO ONE, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 28, 2018, Line 8, PLAINTIFF ALTEN CONSTRUCTION, INC.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION Plaintiff Alten Construction, Inc.'s motion for summary adjudication on the issue that defendant Fast Wrap Reno One, LLC had a duty to defend Alten in the underlying action is granted. The District's claims alleging that the containment barrier was improperly installed by Fast Wrap falls within the scope of the indemnification language in the subcontract between Alten and Fast Wrap. While the indemnity language does not explicitly state Fast Wrap has a "duty to defend," the duty to defend is broader than the duty to indemnify and requires the indemnitor to defend any claims that allege facts that would potentially be covered by the indemnification provision. (Crawford v. Weather Shield Mfg., Inc. (2008) 44 Cal.4th 541; Civil Code 2778(4)). The fact that the indemnification provision does not use the word "defend," while the unrelated Section 15 entitled "Use of Contractor's Equipment" uses the word "defend" is not clear evidence of the parties' mutual intent to disclaim Fast Wrap's duty to defend under the indemnification provision. Contrary to Fast Wrap's argument, section 13 of the subcontract is not an exculpatory provision that relieves Fast Wrap's of its duty to defend. Civil Code 2782.05(a) does not relieve Fast Wrap from its duty to defend even if, assuming without deciding, Fast Wrap provided sufficient evidence showing the District's claims arose from Alten's active negligence and/or willful misconduct. Section 2782.05(a) does not expressly affect a duty to defend. Section 2782.05 is an expansion of Civil Code 2782 since section 2782 rendered indemnification provisions inapplicable where the subcontractor was required to indemnify for claims arising out of the general contractor's sole negligence or willful misconduct while section 2782.05 expanded this to the general contractor's active negligence. Although there is limited case law discussing section 2782.05, a footnote 44 in Crawford states that section 2782 does not relieve an indemnitor from its duty to defend in the indemnity context. Alten's reply memorandum abandoned the portion of the motion seeking summary adjudication on the duty to indemnify. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Alten is required to prepare a proposed order which complies with CCP 437c(g) and repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: SAN FRANCISCO INDEPENDENT TAX ASSOCIATION VS. MOHAMMAD IQBAL KHAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Entry Of Default
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 28, 2018, Line 10, DEFENDANT MOHAMMAD KHAN's Motion To Set Aside Entry Of Default Defendant Mohammad Khan's motion to set aside the default entered against him on March 22, 2018 is granted. The default is now set aside and Mr. Khan must file an answer no later than September 7, 2018. Mr. Khan has presented sufficient evidence to support setting aside the default per CCP 473(b) and 473.5. Granting the motion comports with California's strong policy of deciding cases on their merits and plaintiff San Francisco Independent Taxi Association has not argued, much less shown, that it would suffer legal prejudice by the granting of this motion. While the motion could have been filed earlier, Mr. Khan acted with sufficient diligence by having his counsel contact plaintiff's counsel approximately two months after Mr. Khan reviewed the complaint. Plaintiff's argument that Mr. Khan received notice of this lawsuit in sufficient time to respond to the complaint is based on inadmissible speculation and, even if accepted, would not preclude relief per CCP 473(b). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Khan is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

Mr. Chevalier must file his supplemental opposition memorandum by September 12, 2018. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: ROSS H. MOORE VS. UNICORN CLUB, LTD. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Unicorn Club, Ltds Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 28, 2018, Line 2, DEFENDANT UNICORN CLUB, LTD.'S Motion To Compel Further Responses And For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Stephanie Krmpotic, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant's Request for Production of Documents, No. 1 - deny. Plaintiff has provided a clear response, and the court will not assess the credibility of the parties, No. 2 - grant. The request is reasonable calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. No. 3 - grant, subject to redaction of private information. No. 4 - grant, limited to pages showing arrival and departure dates. No. 5 - grant, as to travel related entries, only. No. 6 - grant. No. 7 - 9, deny. These requests are overbroad and unduly burdensome. The parties are to meet and confer to narrow the scope of the information requested. No. 10 - 11 - grant, subject to redaction of private information. No. 15 - grant, as to statements related to travel, only. All other information is subject to redaction. No. 16 - grant, as to communication related to travel, only. Plaintiff is to provide further responses and documents within 20-days of the order. Request for Admissions: Defendant's motion is denied. The responses are clear and straightforward. Responses to Form Interrogatory, 17.1 - grant. Responses (b) - (d) fail to address the responses to the Requests for Admissions and lack specificity regarding witnesses and documents. Plaintiff is to provide further responses within 20-days of the order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to JSKrmpotic@LOWBALL.COM with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: EDDY CABRERA FRANCO VS. VENDOR DIRECT SOLUTIONS, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Re Mtn To Quash And For An Award Of A Monetary Sanction Against Defts And Their Counsel Denise Trani-Morris In The Amount Of $2380
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 28, 2018, Line 4, PLAINTIFF EDDY FRANCO'S Motion To Quash And For An Award Of A Monetary Sanction Against Defts And Their Counsel Denise Trani-Morris In The Amount Of $2380. Pro Tem Judge Stephanie Krmpotic, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's motion is granted in part. Both parties cite Williams v. Superior Court (2017) 3 Cal.5th 531 for the standard to be applied. Accordingly, the subpoena issued by defendants to prior employers, Ikon and Kirkland & Ellis shall be limited to job description, application for employment and dates of employment and non-private information. Plaintiff is to provide a privilege log. The Subpoena to plaintiff's subsequent employer, Canon, shall be limited to job description, compensation, earnings, and benefits. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to JSKrmpotic@LOWBALL.COM with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: SAN FRANCISCO FEDERAL CREDIT UNION VS. SAN FRANCISCO MUNICIPAL TRANSPORTATION AGENCY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 28, 2018, Line 12, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendant City and County of San Francisco's demurrer to all six causes of action in plaintiff San Francisco Federal Credit Union's first amended complaint is overruled as to the first, second and third causes of action for breach of contract, breach of the implied covenant and breach of a mandatory duty and sustained without leave to amend as to the fourth, fifth and sixth causes of action for breach of fiduciary duty, negligent misrepresentation and promissory estoppel. The Credit Union sufficiently alleges claims for breach of contract and breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. "While plaintiffs' interpretation of the contract may prove invalid, it [is] improper to resolve the issue against her solely on her own pleading. 'In ruling on a demurrer, the likelihood that the pleading will be able to prove his allegations is not the question.'" (Aragon-Haas v. Family Security Ins. Services, Inc., (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 232, 239). The broad language of the contract is reasonably susceptible to the Credit Union's interpretation. As to the mandatory duty claim, taking the Credit Union's allegations as true, the Credit Union has sufficiently alleged that the City chose to terminate the transfer program without making payments contrary to its mandatory duty per SF Transportation Code 1116(d)(3). As to the fiduciary duty claim, section 10 of the parties' agreement states that the parties were not creating a joint venture or partnership and the conclusory allegations in the complaint do not show a fiduciary relationship. As to the negligent misrepresentation claim, the Credit Union's reliance on Arthur L. Sachs, Inc. v. Oceanside (1984) 151 Cal.App.3d 315, 320 is misplaced. That case held that immunity from liability for employee's misrepresentation per Government Code 818.8 was no bar to a contract action for rescission under Government Code 814. The Credit Union purports to allege a distinct tort claim for damages and is not seeking rescission. A plaintiff cannot state a claim for promissory estoppel when the promise was given in return for proper consideration. The claim instead must be pleaded as one for breach of the bargained-for contract. (Raedeke v. Gibraltar Savings & Loan Assn. (1974) 10 Cal.3d 665, 672-673; see Garcia v. World Savings, FSB (2010) 183 Cal.App.4th 1031, 1040-1041.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the City is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk
